{{Shrine |Name= Suwa Shrine (Nakatsugawa, Yamaguchi) (諏訪神社) |image = {{Image requested|cat=Nakatsugawa, Gifu}} |location= {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan |coordinates= {{Coord|35|33|47|N|137|32|24|E|region:JP-21_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}}, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}, {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} |established= |main_hall= |festival= |shinto_rituals= }}
'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a **Shinto shrine** located in {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan.
The shrine was founded as a branch shrine of **Suwa Taisha** in **Shinano Province** and enshrines a sacred mirror as its object of worship. The shrine crest is **Kaji no Ha** (梶の葉).
== Enshrined Deities == === Main Deity === * {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}
=== Additional Deities === * {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}} * {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Toyouke-hime-no-Kami|ja|豊受姫命}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|ja|大山祇命}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|ja|火産霊神}} * {{ill|Sarutahiko-no-Kami|ja|猿田彦命}}
== History == A surviving **munafuda** (棟札, ridge plaque) records that the shrine underwent repairs in **Keichō 16 (1611)**, indicating that it was founded even earlier.
A massive **cryptomeria tree**, with a circumference of **8 meters**, is located within the shrine precincts, demonstrating its long history.
According to the **munafuda**, the current **honden** (main hall) was rebuilt in **Bunka 4 (1801)** by master carpenter **Nakamura Denzaemon Zenju** from **Miyanokoshi-juku**. It is made entirely of **keyaki wood** and features exquisite carvings of **eagles, cranes, dragons, koi fish, immortals, and figures from Takasago**.
In **Meiji 5 (1872)**, the shrine was designated a **village shrine** (村社), and in **Meiji 41 (1908)**, the nearby **Sarutobi Shrine** (founded in Keichō 16) was merged into it.
A further **merger in Meiji 42 (1909)** incorporated deities such as **Izanagi, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime, Toyouke-hime, Susanoo, Ōkuninushi, Kagutsuchi, and Sarutahiko**.
Before World War II, it was designated a **shinsen-heihaku-ryō shrine**, meaning it received state-sponsored offerings during **Kinensai**, **Niinamesai**, and **Reisai** ceremonies.
Following the **Shinto Directive** of **Shōwa 21 (1946)**, the shrine transitioned into a **religious corporation**.
A **400-year-old cryptomeria tree**, which once stood **53 meters tall** and had a **circumference of 3.8 meters**, was cut down in **Shōwa 40 (1965)** to expand **Route 19**.
The area was originally part of **Yamaguchi Village, Kiso District, Nagano Prefecture**, but due to a **cross-prefectural merger on February 13, 2005**, it is now part of **Nakatsugawa City, Gifu Prefecture**.
On **June 3, 1993 (Heisei 5)**, the **Yamaguchi Village Board of Education** designated the **honden** as a **tangible cultural property**, which is now registered under **Nakatsugawa City**.
== Festivals == The annual festival is held on **July 26 and 27**, with the **main festival (Honmatsuri) on July 27** featuring the **Hanamatsuri (Flower Horse Festival)**.
From **July 20**, two villages within the former **Yamaguchi Village** take turns crafting **flower decorations** for the horses.
Records indicate that in **Kyōhō 2 (1717)**, the **Hanamatsuri** was already being held at **Nangū Shrine in Tadai Village**, suggesting that the practice was introduced to the area at that time.
The **flower decorations** mimic **chrysanthemums** and **susuki grass**, with each household receiving one for **pest control in their fields**.
During the two-day festival, **4th and 5th grade girls** perform a **sacred dance** that has been passed down since the **Edo period**.
Before **World War II**, young men undergoing their **military draft examination** were responsible for **organizing the festival**.
On **July 26**, the **portable shrine (mikoshi)** is paraded around the shrine precinct five times, and on **July 27**, it is carried seven times.
== Insect Exorcism (Mushi Okuri) == Until **Shōwa 32 (1957)**, an **insect-exorcism ritual** was performed on **July 28**, the day after the festival.
Farmers would capture **pests such as stink bugs, fleas, flies, and rice weevils**, wrap them in **paper along with rice grains**, and tie them to **bamboo branches** adorned with **colored paper strips**.
The **bamboo was then carried to Suwa Shrine** for purification before being thrown into the **Kiso River from Amagoi Bridge**.
The practice was abandoned due to the **construction of Yamaguchi Dam**, which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about **pollution**.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a **kaguraden**, **worship hall**, and **Awa Shrine**.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features **warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki**, despite its **inland, cold-climate location**.
A **rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)** is also present.
In **Taishō 14 (1925)**, a teacher from **Matsumoto Women's Normal School** declared it a **one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection**, submitting a petition to the **Ministry of Home Affairs**.
Originally designated a **Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture**, the shrine forest was **transferred to Gifu Prefecture** after the **2005 merger**, and was officially designated as a **Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006**.
== References == * **Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2** (1995) * **Shinano Temples & Shrines** (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]
{{shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name= Suwa Shrine (Nakatsugawa, Yamaguchi) (諏訪神社) |image = {{Image requested|cat=Nakatsugawa, Gifu}} |location= {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan |coordinates= {{Coord|35|33|47|N|137|32|24|E|region:JP-21_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}}, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}, {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} |established= |main_hall= |festival= |shinto_rituals= }}
'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan.
The shrine was founded as a branch shrine of '''Suwa Taisha''' in '''Shinano Province''' and enshrines a sacred mirror as its object of worship. The shrine crest is '''Kaji no Ha''' (梶の葉).
== Enshrined Deities == === Main Deity === * {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}
=== Additional Deities === * {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}} * {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Toyouke-hime-no-Kami|ja|豊受姫命}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|ja|大山祇命}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|ja|火産霊神}} * {{ill|Sarutahiko-no-Kami|ja|猿田彦命}}
== History == A surviving '''munafuda''' (棟札, ridge plaque) records that the shrine underwent repairs in '''Keichō 16 (1611)''', indicating that it was founded even earlier.
A massive '''cryptomeria tree''', with a circumference of '''8 meters''', is located within the shrine precincts, demonstrating its long history.
According to the '''munafuda''', the current '''honden''' (main hall) was rebuilt in '''Bunka 4 (1801)''' by master carpenter '''Nakamura Denzaemon Zenju''' from '''Miyanokoshi-juku'''. It is made entirely of '''keyaki wood''' and features exquisite carvings of '''eagles, cranes, dragons, koi fish, immortals, and figures from Takasago'''.
In '''Meiji 5 (1872)''', the shrine was designated a '''village shrine''' (村社), and in '''Meiji 41 (1908)''', the nearby '''Sarutobi Shrine''' (founded in Keichō 16) was merged into it.
A further '''merger in Meiji 42 (1909)''' incorporated deities such as '''Izanagi, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime, Toyouke-hime, Susanoo, Ōkuninushi, Kagutsuchi, and Sarutahiko'''.
Before World War II, it was designated a '''shinsen-heihaku-ryō shrine''', meaning it received state-sponsored offerings during '''Kinensai''', '''Niinamesai''', and '''Reisai''' ceremonies.
Following the '''Shinto Directive''' of '''Shōwa 21 (1946)''', the shrine transitioned into a '''religious corporation'''.
A '''400-year-old cryptomeria tree''', which once stood '''53 meters tall''' and had a '''circumference of 3.8 meters''', was cut down in '''Shōwa 40 (1965)''' to expand '''Route 19'''.
The area was originally part of '''Yamaguchi Village, Kiso District, Nagano Prefecture''', but due to a '''cross-prefectural merger on February 13, 2005''', it is now part of '''Nakatsugawa City, Gifu Prefecture'''.
On '''June 3, 1993 (Heisei 5)''', the '''Yamaguchi Village Board of Education''' designated the '''honden''' as a '''tangible cultural property''', which is now registered under '''Nakatsugawa City'''.
== Festivals == The annual festival is held on '''July 26 and 27''', with the '''main festival (Honmatsuri) on July 27''' featuring the '''Hanamatsuri (Flower Horse Festival)'''.
From '''July 20''', two villages within the former '''Yamaguchi Village''' take turns crafting '''flower decorations''' for the horses.
Records indicate that in '''Kyōhō 2 (1717)''', the '''Hanamatsuri''' was already being held at '''Nangū Shrine in Tadai Village''', suggesting that the practice was introduced to the area at that time.
The '''flower decorations''' mimic '''chrysanthemums''' and '''susuki grass''', with each household receiving one for '''pest control in their fields'''.
During the two-day festival, '''4th and 5th grade girls''' perform a '''sacred dance''' that has been passed down since the '''Edo period'''.
Before '''World War II''', young men undergoing their '''military draft examination''' were responsible for '''organizing the festival'''.
On '''July 26''', the '''portable shrine (mikoshi)''' is paraded around the shrine precinct five times, and on '''July 27''', it is carried seven times.
== Insect Exorcism (Mushi Okuri) == Until '''Shōwa 32 (1957)''', an '''insect-exorcism ritual''' was performed on '''July 28''', the day after the festival.
Farmers would capture '''pests such as stink bugs, fleas, flies, and rice weevils''', wrap them in '''paper along with rice grains''', and tie them to '''bamboo branches''' adorned with '''colored paper strips'''.
The '''bamboo was then carried to Suwa Shrine''' for purification before being thrown into the '''Kiso River from Amagoi Bridge'''.
The practice was abandoned due to the '''construction of Yamaguchi Dam''', which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about '''pollution'''.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a '''kaguraden''', '''worship hall''', and '''Awa Shrine'''.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features '''warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki''', despite its '''inland, cold-climate location'''.
A '''rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)''' is also present.
In '''Taishō 14 (1925)''', a teacher from '''Matsumoto Women's Normal School''' declared it a '''one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection''', submitting a petition to the '''Ministry of Home Affairs'''.
Originally designated a '''Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture''', the shrine forest was '''transferred to Gifu Prefecture''' after the '''2005 merger''', and was officially designated as a '''Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006'''.
== References == * '''Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2''' (1995) * '''Shinano Temples & Shrines''' (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]

From '''July 20''', two villages within the former '''Yamaguchi Village''' take turns crafting '''flower decorations''' for the horses.

The '''flower decorations''' mimic '''chrysanthemums''' and '''susuki grass''', with each household receiving one for '''pest control in their fields'''.
During the two-day festival, '''4th and 5th grade girls''' perform a '''sacred dance''' that has been passed down since the '''Edo period'''.
Before '''World War II''', young men undergoing their '''military draft examination''' were responsible for '''organizing the festival'''.
On '''July 26''', the '''portable shrine (mikoshi)''' is paraded around the shrine precinct five times, and on '''July 27''', it is carried seven times.
== Insect Exorcism (Mushi Okuri) == Until '''Shōwa 32 (1957)''', an '''insect-exorcism ritual''' was performed on '''July 28''', the day after the festival.
Farmers would capture '''pests such as stink bugs, fleas, flies, and rice weevils''', wrap them in '''paper along with rice grains''', and tie them to '''bamboo branches''' adorned with '''colored paper strips'''.
The '''bamboo was then carried to Suwa Shrine''' for purification before being thrown into the '''Kiso River from Amagoi Bridge'''.
The practice was abandoned due to the '''construction of Yamaguchi Dam''', which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about '''pollution'''.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a '''kaguraden''', '''worship hall''', and '''Awa Shrine'''.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features '''warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki''', despite its '''inland, cold-climate location'''.
A '''rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)''' is also present.
In '''Taishō 14 (1925)''', a teacher from '''Matsumoto Women's Normal School''' declared it a '''one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection''', submitting a petition to the '''Ministry of Home Affairs'''.
Originally designated a '''Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture''', the shrine forest was '''transferred to Gifu Prefecture''' after the '''2005 merger''', and was officially designated as a '''Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006'''.
== References == * '''Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2''' (1995) * '''Shinano Temples & Shrines''' (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]

{{Shrine |Name= Suwa Shrine (Nakatsugawa, Yamaguchi) (諏訪神社) |image = {{image requested|cat=Nakatsugawa, Gifu}} |location= {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan |coordinates= {{Coord|35|33|47|N|137|32|24|E|region:JP-21_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}}, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}, {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} |established= |main_hall= |festival= |shinto_rituals= }}
'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan.
The shrine was founded as a branch shrine of '''Suwa Taisha''' in '''Shinano Province''' and enshrines a sacred mirror as its object of worship. The shrine crest is '''Kaji no Ha''' (梶の葉).
== Enshrined Deities == === Main deity === * {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}
=== Additional Deities === * {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}} * {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Toyouke-hime-no-Kami|ja|豊受姫命}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|ja|大山祇命}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|ja|火産霊神}} * {{ill|Sarutahiko-no-Kami|ja|猿田彦命}}
== History == A surviving '''munafuda''' (棟札, ridge plaque) records that the shrine underwent repairs in '''Keichō 16 (1611)''', indicating that it was founded even earlier.
A massive '''cryptomeria tree''', with a circumference of '''8 meters''', is located within the shrine precincts, demonstrating its long history.
According to the '''munafuda''', the current '''honden''' (main hall) was rebuilt in '''Bunka 4 (1801)''' by master carpenter '''Nakamura Denzaemon Zenju''' from '''Miyanokoshi-juku'''. It is made entirely of '''keyaki wood''' and features exquisite carvings of '''eagles, cranes, dragons, koi fish, immortals, and figures from Takasago'''.
In '''Meiji 5 (1872)''', the shrine was designated a '''village shrine''' (村社), and in '''Meiji 41 (1908)''', the nearby '''Sarutobi Shrine''' (founded in Keichō 16) was merged into it.
A further '''merger in Meiji 42 (1909)''' incorporated deities such as '''Izanagi, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime, Toyouke-hime, Susanoo, Ōkuninushi, Kagutsuchi, and Sarutahiko'''.
Before World War II, it was designated a '''shinsen-heihaku-ryō shrine''', meaning it received state-sponsored offerings during '''Kinensai''', '''Niinamesai''', and '''Reisai''' ceremonies.
Following the '''Shinto Directive''' of '''Shōwa 21 (1946)''', the shrine transitioned into a '''religious corporation'''.
A '''400-year-old cryptomeria tree''', which once stood '''53 meters tall''' and had a '''circumference of 3.8 meters''', was cut down in '''Shōwa 40 (1965)''' to expand '''Route 19'''.
The area was originally part of '''Yamaguchi Village, Kiso District, Nagano Prefecture''', but due to a '''cross-prefectural merger on February 13, 2005''', it is now part of '''Nakatsugawa City, Gifu Prefecture'''.
On '''June 3, 1993 (Heisei 5)''', the '''Yamaguchi Village Board of Education''' designated the '''honden''' as a '''tangible cultural property''', which is now registered under '''Nakatsugawa City'''.
== Festivals == The annual festival is held on '''July 26 and 27''', with the '''main festival (Honmatsuri) on July 27''' featuring the '''Hanamatsuri (Flower Horse Festival)'''.
From '''July 20''', two villages within the former '''Yamaguchi Village''' take turns crafting '''flower decorations''' for the horses.
Records indicate that in '''Kyōhō 2 (1717)''', the '''Hanamatsuri''' was already being held at '''Nangū Shrine in Tadai Village''', suggesting that the practice was introduced to the area at that time.
The '''flower decorations''' mimic '''chrysanthemums''' and '''susuki grass''', with each household receiving one for '''pest control in their fields'''.
During the two-day festival, '''4th and 5th grade girls''' perform a '''sacred dance''' that has been passed down since the '''Edo period'''.
Before '''World War II''', young men undergoing their '''military draft examination''' were responsible for '''organizing the festival'''.
On '''July 26''', the '''portable shrine (mikoshi)''' is paraded around the shrine precinct five times, and on '''July 27''', it is carried seven times.
== Insect Exorcism (Mushi Okuri) == Until '''Shōwa 32 (1957)''', an '''insect-exorcism ritual''' was performed on '''July 28''', the day after the festival.
Farmers would capture '''pests such as stink bugs, fleas, flies, and rice weevils''', wrap them in '''paper along with rice grains''', and tie them to '''bamboo branches''' adorned with '''colored paper strips'''.
The '''bamboo was then carried to Suwa Shrine''' for purification before being thrown into the '''Kiso River from Amagoi Bridge'''.
The practice was abandoned due to the '''construction of Yamaguchi Dam''', which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about '''pollution'''.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a '''kaguraden''', '''worship hall''', and '''Awa Shrine'''.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features '''warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki''', despite its '''inland, cold-climate location'''.
A '''rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)''' is also present.
In '''Taishō 14 (1925)''', a teacher from '''Matsumoto Women's Normal School''' declared it a '''one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection''', submitting a petition to the '''Ministry of Home Affairs'''.
Originally designated a '''Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture''', the shrine forest was '''transferred to Gifu Prefecture''' after the '''2005 merger''', and was officially designated as a '''Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006'''.
== References == * '''Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2''' (1995) * '''Shinano Temples & Shrines''' (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]

=== Additional Deities === * {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}} * {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Toyouke-hime-no-Kami|ja|豊受姫命}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|ja|大山祇命}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|ja|火産霊神}} * {{ill|Sarutahiko-no-Kami|ja|猿田彦命}}
== History == A surviving '''munafuda''' (棟札, ridge plaque) records that the shrine underwent repairs in '''Keichō 16 (1611)''', indicating that it was founded even earlier.
A massive '''cryptomeria tree''', with a circumference of '''8 meters''', is located within the shrine precincts, demonstrating its long history.
According to the '''munafuda''', the current '''honden''' (main hall) was rebuilt in '''Bunka 4 (1801)''' by master carpenter '''Nakamura Denzaemon Zenju''' from '''Miyanokoshi-juku'''. It is made entirely of '''keyaki wood''' and features exquisite carvings of '''eagles, cranes, dragons, koi fish, immortals, and figures from Takasago'''.
In '''Meiji 5 (1872)''', the shrine was designated a '''village shrine''' (村社), and in '''Meiji 41 (1908)''', the nearby '''Sarutobi Shrine''' (founded in Keichō 16) was merged into it.
A further '''merger in Meiji 42 (1909)''' incorporated deities such as '''Izanagi, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime, Toyouke-hime, Susanoo, Ōkuninushi, Kagutsuchi, and Sarutahiko'''.
Before World War II, it was designated a '''shinsen-heihaku-ryō shrine''', meaning it received state-sponsored offerings during '''Kinensai''', '''Niinamesai''', and '''Reisai''' ceremonies.
Following the '''Shinto Directive''' of '''Shōwa 21 (1946)''', the shrine transitioned into a '''religious corporation'''.
A '''400-year-old cryptomeria tree''', which once stood '''53 meters tall''' and had a '''circumference of 3.8 meters''', was cut down in '''Shōwa 40 (1965)''' to expand '''Route 19'''.
The area was originally part of '''Yamaguchi Village, Kiso District, Nagano Prefecture''', but due to a '''cross-prefectural merger on February 13, 2005''', it is now part of '''Nakatsugawa City, Gifu Prefecture'''.
On '''June 3, 1993 (Heisei 5)''', the '''Yamaguchi Village Board of Education''' designated the '''honden''' as a '''tangible cultural property''', which is now registered under '''Nakatsugawa City'''.
== Festivals == The annual festival is held on '''July 26 and 27''', with the '''main festival (Honmatsuri) on July 27''' featuring the '''Hanamatsuri (Flower Horse Festival)'''.
From '''July 20''', two villages within the former '''Yamaguchi Village''' take turns crafting '''flower decorations''' for the horses.
Records indicate that in '''Kyōhō 2 (1717)''', the '''Hanamatsuri''' was already being held at '''Nangū Shrine in Tadai Village''', suggesting that the practice was introduced to the area at that time.
The '''flower decorations''' mimic '''chrysanthemums''' and '''susuki grass''', with each household receiving one for '''pest control in their fields'''.
During the two-day festival, '''4th and 5th grade girls''' perform a '''sacred dance''' that has been passed down since the '''Edo period'''.
Before '''World War II''', young men undergoing their '''military draft examination''' were responsible for '''organizing the festival'''.
On '''July 26''', the '''portable shrine (mikoshi)''' is paraded around the shrine precinct five times, and on '''July 27''', it is carried seven times.
== Insect Exorcism (Mushi Okuri) == Until '''Shōwa 32 (1957)''', an '''insect-exorcism ritual''' was performed on '''July 28''', the day after the festival.
Farmers would capture '''pests such as stink bugs, fleas, flies, and rice weevils''', wrap them in '''paper along with rice grains''', and tie them to '''bamboo branches''' adorned with '''colored paper strips'''.
The '''bamboo was then carried to Suwa Shrine''' for purification before being thrown into the '''Kiso River from Amagoi Bridge'''.
The practice was abandoned due to the '''construction of Yamaguchi Dam''', which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about '''pollution'''.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a '''kaguraden''', '''worship hall''', and '''Awa Shrine'''.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features '''warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki''', despite its '''inland, cold-climate location'''.
A '''rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)''' is also present.
In '''Taishō 14 (1925)''', a teacher from '''Matsumoto Women's Normal School''' declared it a '''one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection''', submitting a petition to the '''Ministry of Home Affairs'''.
Originally designated a '''Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture''', the shrine forest was '''transferred to Gifu Prefecture''' after the '''2005 merger''', and was officially designated as a '''Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006'''.
== References == * '''Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2''' (1995) * '''Shinano Temples & Shrines''' (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]

The practice was abandoned due to the '''construction of Yamaguchi Dam''', which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about '''pollution'''.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a '''kaguraden''', '''worship hall''', and '''Awa Shrine'''.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features '''warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki''', despite its '''inland, cold-climate location'''.
A '''rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)''' is also present.
In '''Taishō 14 (1925)''', a teacher from '''Matsumoto Women's Normal School''' declared it a '''one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection''', submitting a petition to the '''Ministry of Home Affairs'''.
Originally designated a '''Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture''', the shrine forest was '''transferred to Gifu Prefecture''' after the '''2005 merger''', and was officially designated as a '''Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006'''.
== References == * '''Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2''' (1995) * '''Shinano Temples & Shrines''' (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]

{{Shrine |Name= Suwa Shrine (Nakatsugawa, Yamaguchi) (諏訪神社) |image = {{image requested|cat=Nakatsugawa, Gifu}} |location= {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan |coordinates= {{Coord|35|33|47|N|137|32|24|E|region:JP-21_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}}, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}, {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} |established= |main_hall= |festival= |shinto_rituals= }}
'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan.
The shrine was founded as a branch shrine of '''Suwa Taisha''' in '''Shinano Province''' and enshrines a sacred mirror as its object of worship. The shrine crest is '''Kaji no Ha''' (梶の葉).
== Enshrined Deities == === Main deity === * {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}
=== Additional Deities === * {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}} * {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Toyouke-hime-no-Kami|ja|豊受姫命}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|ja|大山祇命}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|ja|火産霊神}} * {{ill|Sarutahiko-no-Kami|ja|猿田彦命}}
== History == A surviving '''munafuda''' (棟札, ridge plaque) records that the shrine underwent repairs in '''Keichō 16 (1611)''', indicating that it was founded even earlier.
A massive '''cryptomeria tree''', with a circumference of '''8 meters''', is located within the shrine precincts, demonstrating its long history.
According to the '''munafuda''', the current '''honden''' (main hall) was rebuilt in '''Bunka 4 (1801)''' by master carpenter '''Nakamura Denzaemon Zenju''' from '''Miyanokoshi-juku'''. It is made entirely of '''keyaki wood''' and features exquisite carvings of '''eagles, cranes, dragons, koi fish, immortals, and figures from Takasago'''.
In '''Meiji 5 (1872)''', the shrine was designated a '''village shrine''' (村社), and in '''Meiji 41 (1908)''', the nearby '''Sarutobi Shrine''' (founded in Keichō 16) was merged into it.
A further '''merger in Meiji 42 (1909)''' incorporated deities such as '''Izanagi, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime, Toyouke-hime, Susanoo, Ōkuninushi, Kagutsuchi, and Sarutahiko'''.
Before World War II, it was designated a '''shinsen-heihaku-ryō shrine''', meaning it received state-sponsored offerings during '''Kinensai''', '''Niinamesai''', and '''Reisai''' ceremonies.
Following the '''Shinto Directive''' of '''Shōwa 21 (1946)''', the shrine transitioned into a '''religious corporation'''.
A '''400-year-old cryptomeria tree''', which once stood '''53 meters tall''' and had a '''circumference of 3.8 meters''', was cut down in '''Shōwa 40 (1965)''' to expand '''Route 19'''.
The area was originally part of '''Yamaguchi Village, Kiso District, Nagano Prefecture''', but due to a '''cross-prefectural merger on February 13, 2005''', it is now part of '''Nakatsugawa City, Gifu Prefecture'''.
On '''June 3, 1993 (Heisei 5)''', the '''Yamaguchi Village Board of Education''' designated the '''honden''' as a '''tangible cultural property''', which is now registered under '''Nakatsugawa City'''.
== Festivals == The annual festival is held on '''July 26 and 27''', with the '''main festival (Honmatsuri) on July 27''' featuring the '''Hanamatsuri (Flower Horse Festival)'''.
From '''July 20''', two villages within the former '''Yamaguchi Village''' take turns crafting '''flower decorations''' for the horses.
Records indicate that in '''Kyōhō 2 (1717)''', the '''Hanamatsuri''' was already being held at '''Nangū Shrine in Tadai Village''', suggesting that the practice was introduced to the area at that time.
The '''flower decorations''' mimic '''chrysanthemums''' and '''susuki grass''', with each household receiving one for '''pest control in their fields'''.
During the two-day festival, '''4th and 5th grade girls''' perform a '''sacred dance''' that has been passed down since the '''Edo period'''.
Before '''World War II''', young men undergoing their '''military draft examination''' were responsible for '''organizing the festival'''.
On '''July 26''', the '''portable shrine (mikoshi)''' is paraded around the shrine precinct five times, and on '''July 27''', it is carried seven times.
== Insect Exorcism (Mushi Okuri) == Until '''Shōwa 32 (1957)''', an '''insect-exorcism ritual''' was performed on '''July 28''', the day after the festival.
Farmers would capture '''pests such as stink bugs, fleas, flies, and rice weevils''', wrap them in '''paper along with rice grains''', and tie them to '''bamboo branches''' adorned with '''colored paper strips'''.
The '''bamboo was then carried to Suwa Shrine''' for purification before being thrown into the '''Kiso River from Amagoi Bridge'''.
The practice was abandoned due to the '''construction of Yamaguchi Dam''', which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about '''pollution'''.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a '''kaguraden''', '''worship hall''', and '''Awa Shrine'''.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features '''warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki''', despite its '''inland, cold-climate location'''.
A '''rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)''' is also present.
In '''Taishō 14 (1925)''', a teacher from '''Matsumoto Women's Normal School''' declared it a '''one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection''', submitting a petition to the '''Ministry of Home Affairs'''.
Originally designated a '''Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture''', the shrine forest was '''transferred to Gifu Prefecture''' after the '''2005 merger''', and was officially designated as a '''Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006'''.
== References == * '''Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2''' (1995) * '''Shinano Temples & Shrines''' (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]

The shrine was founded as a branch shrine of '''Suwa Taisha''' in '''Shinano Province''' and enshrines a sacred mirror as its object of worship. The shrine crest is '''Kaji no Ha''' (梶の葉).
== Enshrined Deities == === Main deity === * {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}
=== Additional Deities === * {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}} * {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Toyouke-hime-no-Kami|ja|豊受姫命}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|ja|大山祇命}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|ja|火産霊神}} * {{ill|Sarutahiko-no-Kami|ja|猿田彦命}}
== History == A surviving '''munafuda''' (棟札, ridge plaque) records that the shrine underwent repairs in '''Keichō 16 (1611)''', indicating that it was founded even earlier.
A massive '''cryptomeria tree''', with a circumference of '''8 meters''', is located within the shrine precincts, demonstrating its long history.
According to the '''munafuda''', the current '''honden''' (main hall) was rebuilt in '''Bunka 4 (1801)''' by master carpenter '''Nakamura Denzaemon Zenju''' from '''Miyanokoshi-juku'''. It is made entirely of '''keyaki wood''' and features exquisite carvings of '''eagles, cranes, dragons, koi fish, immortals, and figures from Takasago'''.
In '''Meiji 5 (1872)''', the shrine was designated a '''village shrine''' (村社), and in '''Meiji 41 (1908)''', the nearby '''Sarutobi Shrine''' (founded in Keichō 16) was merged into it.
A further '''merger in Meiji 42 (1909)''' incorporated deities such as '''Izanagi, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime, Toyouke-hime, Susanoo, Ōkuninushi, Kagutsuchi, and Sarutahiko'''.
Before World War II, it was designated a '''shinsen-heihaku-ryō shrine''', meaning it received state-sponsored offerings during '''Kinensai''', '''Niinamesai''', and '''Reisai''' ceremonies.
Following the '''Shinto Directive''' of '''Shōwa 21 (1946)''', the shrine transitioned into a '''religious corporation'''.
A '''400-year-old cryptomeria tree''', which once stood '''53 meters tall''' and had a '''circumference of 3.8 meters''', was cut down in '''Shōwa 40 (1965)''' to expand '''Route 19'''.
The area was originally part of '''Yamaguchi Village, Kiso District, Nagano Prefecture''', but due to a '''cross-prefectural merger on February 13, 2005''', it is now part of '''Nakatsugawa City, Gifu Prefecture'''.
On '''June 3, 1993 (Heisei 5)''', the '''Yamaguchi Village Board of Education''' designated the '''honden''' as a '''tangible cultural property''', which is now registered under '''Nakatsugawa City'''.
== Festivals == The annual festival is held on '''July 26 and 27''', with the '''main festival (Honmatsuri) on July 27''' featuring the '''Hanamatsuri (Flower Horse Festival)'''.
From '''July 20''', two villages within the former '''Yamaguchi Village''' take turns crafting '''flower decorations''' for the horses.
Records indicate that in '''Kyōhō 2 (1717)''', the '''Hanamatsuri''' was already being held at '''Nangū Shrine in Tadai Village''', suggesting that the practice was introduced to the area at that time.
The '''flower decorations''' mimic '''chrysanthemums''' and '''susuki grass''', with each household receiving one for '''pest control in their fields'''.
During the two-day festival, '''4th and 5th grade girls''' perform a '''sacred dance''' that has been passed down since the '''Edo period'''.
Before '''World War II''', young men undergoing their '''military draft examination''' were responsible for '''organizing the festival'''.
On '''July 26''', the '''portable shrine (mikoshi)''' is paraded around the shrine precinct five times, and on '''July 27''', it is carried seven times.
== Insect Exorcism (Mushi Okuri) == Until '''Shōwa 32 (1957)''', an '''insect-exorcism ritual''' was performed on '''July 28''', the day after the festival.
Farmers would capture '''pests such as stink bugs, fleas, flies, and rice weevils''', wrap them in '''paper along with rice grains''', and tie them to '''bamboo branches''' adorned with '''colored paper strips'''.
The '''bamboo was then carried to Suwa Shrine''' for purification before being thrown into the '''Kiso River from Amagoi Bridge'''.
The practice was abandoned due to the '''construction of Yamaguchi Dam''', which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about '''pollution'''.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a '''kaguraden''', '''worship hall''', and '''Awa Shrine'''.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features '''warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki''', despite its '''inland, cold-climate location'''.
A '''rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)''' is also present.
In '''Taishō 14 (1925)''', a teacher from '''Matsumoto Women's Normal School''' declared it a '''one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection''', submitting a petition to the '''Ministry of Home Affairs'''.
Originally designated a '''Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture''', the shrine forest was '''transferred to Gifu Prefecture''' after the '''2005 merger''', and was officially designated as a '''Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006'''.
== References == * '''Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2''' (1995) * '''Shinano Temples & Shrines''' (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]

Records indicate that in '''Kyōhō 2 (1717)''', the '''Hanamatsuri''' was already being held at '''Nangū Shrine in Tadai Village''', suggesting that the practice was introduced to the area at that time.
The '''flower decorations''' mimic '''chrysanthemums''' and '''susuki grass''', with each household receiving one for '''pest control in their fields'''.
During the two-day festival, '''4th and 5th grade girls''' perform a '''sacred dance''' that has been passed down since the '''Edo period'''.
Before '''World War II''', young men undergoing their '''military draft examination''' were responsible for '''organizing the festival'''.
On '''July 26''', the '''portable shrine (mikoshi)''' is paraded around the shrine precinct five times, and on '''July 27''', it is carried seven times.
== Insect Exorcism (Mushi Okuri) == Until '''Shōwa 32 (1957)''', an '''insect-exorcism ritual''' was performed on '''July 28''', the day after the festival.
Farmers would capture '''pests such as stink bugs, fleas, flies, and rice weevils''', wrap them in '''paper along with rice grains''', and tie them to '''bamboo branches''' adorned with '''colored paper strips'''.
The '''bamboo was then carried to Suwa Shrine''' for purification before being thrown into the '''Kiso River from Amagoi Bridge'''.
The practice was abandoned due to the '''construction of Yamaguchi Dam''', which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about '''pollution'''.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a '''kaguraden''', '''worship hall''', and '''Awa Shrine'''.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features '''warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki''', despite its '''inland, cold-climate location'''.
A '''rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)''' is also present.
In '''Taishō 14 (1925)''', a teacher from '''Matsumoto Women's Normal School''' declared it a '''one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection''', submitting a petition to the '''Ministry of Home Affairs'''.
Originally designated a '''Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture''', the shrine forest was '''transferred to Gifu Prefecture''' after the '''2005 merger''', and was officially designated as a '''Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006'''.
== References == * '''Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2''' (1995) * '''Shinano Temples & Shrines''' (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]

{{Shrine |Name= Suwa Shrine (Nakatsugawa, Yamaguchi) (諏訪神社) |image = {{image requested|cat=Nakatsugawa, Gifu}} |location= {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan |coordinates= {{Coord|35|33|47|N|137|32|24|E|region:JP-21_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}}, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}, {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} |established= |main_hall= |festival= |shinto_rituals= }}
'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan.
The shrine was founded as a branch shrine of '''Suwa Taisha''' in '''Shinano Province''' and enshrines a sacred mirror as its object of worship. The shrine crest is '''Kaji no Ha''' (梶の葉).
== Enshrined Deities == === Main deity === * {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}
=== Additional Deities === * {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}} * {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Toyouke-hime-no-Kami|ja|豊受姫命}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|ja|火産霊神}} * {{ill|Sarutahiko-no-Kami|ja|猿田彦命}}
== History == A surviving '''munafuda''' (棟札, ridge plaque) records that the shrine underwent repairs in '''Keichō 16 (1611)''', indicating that it was founded even earlier.
A massive '''cryptomeria tree''', with a circumference of '''8 meters''', is located within the shrine precincts, demonstrating its long history.
According to the '''munafuda''', the current '''honden''' (main hall) was rebuilt in '''Bunka 4 (1801)''' by master carpenter '''Nakamura Denzaemon Zenju''' from '''Miyanokoshi-juku'''. It is made entirely of '''keyaki wood''' and features exquisite carvings of '''eagles, cranes, dragons, koi fish, immortals, and figures from Takasago'''.
In '''Meiji 5 (1872)''', the shrine was designated a '''village shrine''' (村社), and in '''Meiji 41 (1908)''', the nearby '''Sarutobi Shrine''' (founded in Keichō 16) was merged into it.
A further '''merger in Meiji 42 (1909)''' incorporated deities such as '''Izanagi, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime, Toyouke-hime, Susanoo, Ōkuninushi, Kagutsuchi, and Sarutahiko'''.
Before World War II, it was designated a '''shinsen-heihaku-ryō shrine''', meaning it received state-sponsored offerings during '''Kinensai''', '''Niinamesai''', and '''Reisai''' ceremonies.
Following the '''Shinto Directive''' of '''Shōwa 21 (1946)''', the shrine transitioned into a '''religious corporation'''.
A '''400-year-old cryptomeria tree''', which once stood '''53 meters tall''' and had a '''circumference of 3.8 meters''', was cut down in '''Shōwa 40 (1965)''' to expand '''Route 19'''.
The area was originally part of '''Yamaguchi Village, Kiso District, Nagano Prefecture''', but due to a '''cross-prefectural merger on February 13, 2005''', it is now part of '''Nakatsugawa City, Gifu Prefecture'''.
On '''June 3, 1993 (Heisei 5)''', the '''Yamaguchi Village Board of Education''' designated the '''honden''' as a '''tangible cultural property''', which is now registered under '''Nakatsugawa City'''.
== Festivals == The annual festival is held on '''July 26 and 27''', with the '''main festival (Honmatsuri) on July 27''' featuring the '''Hanamatsuri (Flower Horse Festival)'''.
From '''July 20''', two villages within the former '''Yamaguchi Village''' take turns crafting '''flower decorations''' for the horses.
Records indicate that in '''Kyōhō 2 (1717)''', the '''Hanamatsuri''' was already being held at '''Nangū Shrine in Tadai Village''', suggesting that the practice was introduced to the area at that time.
The '''flower decorations''' mimic '''chrysanthemums''' and '''susuki grass''', with each household receiving one for '''pest control in their fields'''.
During the two-day festival, '''4th and 5th grade girls''' perform a '''sacred dance''' that has been passed down since the '''Edo period'''.
Before '''World War II''', young men undergoing their '''military draft examination''' were responsible for '''organizing the festival'''.
On '''July 26''', the '''portable shrine (mikoshi)''' is paraded around the shrine precinct five times, and on '''July 27''', it is carried seven times.
== Insect Exorcism (Mushi Okuri) == Until '''Shōwa 32 (1957)''', an '''insect-exorcism ritual''' was performed on '''July 28''', the day after the festival.
Farmers would capture '''pests such as stink bugs, fleas, flies, and rice weevils''', wrap them in '''paper along with rice grains''', and tie them to '''bamboo branches''' adorned with '''colored paper strips'''.
The '''bamboo was then carried to Suwa Shrine''' for purification before being thrown into the '''Kiso River from Amagoi Bridge'''.
The practice was abandoned due to the '''construction of Yamaguchi Dam''', which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about '''pollution'''.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a '''kaguraden''', '''worship hall''', and '''Awa Shrine'''.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features '''warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki''', despite its '''inland, cold-climate location'''.
A '''rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)''' is also present.
In '''Taishō 14 (1925)''', a teacher from '''Matsumoto Women's Normal School''' declared it a '''one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection''', submitting a petition to the '''Ministry of Home Affairs'''.
Originally designated a '''Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture''', the shrine forest was '''transferred to Gifu Prefecture''' after the '''2005 merger''', and was officially designated as a '''Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006'''.
== References == * '''Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2''' (1995) * '''Shinano Temples & Shrines''' (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]

revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name= Suwa Shrine (Nakatsugawa, Yamaguchi) (諏訪神社) |image = {{image requested|cat=Nakatsugawa, Gifu}} |location= {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan |coordinates= {{Coord|35|33|47|N|137|32|24|E|region:JP-21_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}, {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}}, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}, {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} |established= |main_hall= |festival= |shinto_rituals= }}
'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan.
The shrine was founded as a branch shrine of '''Suwa Taisha''' in '''Shinano Province''' and enshrines a sacred mirror as its object of worship. The shrine crest is '''Kaji no Ha''' (梶の葉).
== Enshrined Deities == === Main deity === * {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}
=== Additional Deities === * {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命}} * {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Toyouke-hime-no-Kami|ja|豊受姫命}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|ja|火産霊神}} * {{ill|Sarutahiko-no-Kami|ja|猿田彦命}}
== History == A surviving '''munafuda''' (棟札, ridge plaque) records that the shrine underwent repairs in '''Keichō 16 (1611)''', indicating that it was founded even earlier.
A massive '''cryptomeria tree''', with a circumference of '''8 meters''', is located within the shrine precincts, demonstrating its long history.
According to the '''munafuda''', the current '''honden''' (main hall) was rebuilt in '''Bunka 4 (1801)''' by master carpenter '''Nakamura Denzaemon Zenju''' from '''Miyanokoshi-juku'''. It is made entirely of '''keyaki wood''' and features exquisite carvings of '''eagles, cranes, dragons, koi fish, immortals, and figures from Takasago'''.
In '''Meiji 5 (1872)''', the shrine was designated a '''village shrine''' (村社), and in '''Meiji 41 (1908)''', the nearby '''Sarutobi Shrine''' (founded in Keichō 16) was merged into it.
A further '''merger in Meiji 42 (1909)''' incorporated deities such as '''Izanagi, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime, Toyouke-hime, Susanoo, Ōkuninushi, Kagutsuchi, and Sarutahiko'''.
Before World War II, it was designated a '''shinsen-heihaku-ryō shrine''', meaning it received state-sponsored offerings during '''Kinensai''', '''Niinamesai''', and '''Reisai''' ceremonies.
Following the '''Shinto Directive''' of '''Shōwa 21 (1946)''', the shrine transitioned into a '''religious corporation'''.
A '''400-year-old cryptomeria tree''', which once stood '''53 meters tall''' and had a '''circumference of 3.8 meters''', was cut down in '''Shōwa 40 (1965)''' to expand '''Route 19'''.
The area was originally part of '''Yamaguchi Village, Kiso District, Nagano Prefecture''', but due to a '''cross-prefectural merger on February 13, 2005''', it is now part of '''Nakatsugawa City, Gifu Prefecture'''.
On '''June 3, 1993 (Heisei 5)''', the '''Yamaguchi Village Board of Education''' designated the '''honden''' as a '''tangible cultural property''', which is now registered under '''Nakatsugawa City'''.
== Festivals == The annual festival is held on '''July 26 and 27''', with the '''main festival (Honmatsuri) on July 27''' featuring the '''Hanamatsuri (Flower Horse Festival)'''.
From '''July 20''', two villages within the former '''Yamaguchi Village''' take turns crafting '''flower decorations''' for the horses.
Records indicate that in '''Kyōhō 2 (1717)''', the '''Hanamatsuri''' was already being held at '''Nangū Shrine in Tadai Village''', suggesting that the practice was introduced to the area at that time.
The '''flower decorations''' mimic '''chrysanthemums''' and '''susuki grass''', with each household receiving one for '''pest control in their fields'''.
During the two-day festival, '''4th and 5th grade girls''' perform a '''sacred dance''' that has been passed down since the '''Edo period'''.
Before '''World War II''', young men undergoing their '''military draft examination''' were responsible for '''organizing the festival'''.
On '''July 26''', the '''portable shrine (mikoshi)''' is paraded around the shrine precinct five times, and on '''July 27''', it is carried seven times.
== Insect Exorcism (Mushi Okuri) == Until '''Shōwa 32 (1957)''', an '''insect-exorcism ritual''' was performed on '''July 28''', the day after the festival.
Farmers would capture '''pests such as stink bugs, fleas, flies, and rice weevils''', wrap them in '''paper along with rice grains''', and tie them to '''bamboo branches''' adorned with '''colored paper strips'''.
The '''bamboo was then carried to Suwa Shrine''' for purification before being thrown into the '''Kiso River from Amagoi Bridge'''.
The practice was abandoned due to the '''construction of Yamaguchi Dam''', which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about '''pollution'''.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a '''kaguraden''', '''worship hall''', and '''Awa Shrine'''.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features '''warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki''', despite its '''inland, cold-climate location'''.
A '''rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)''' is also present.
In '''Taishō 14 (1925)''', a teacher from '''Matsumoto Women's Normal School''' declared it a '''one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection''', submitting a petition to the '''Ministry of Home Affairs'''.
Originally designated a '''Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture''', the shrine forest was '''transferred to Gifu Prefecture''' after the '''2005 merger''', and was officially designated as a '''Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006'''.
== References == * '''Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2''' (1995) * '''Shinano Temples & Shrines''' (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]
{{shinto-stub}}

=== Additional Deities === * {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toyouke-hime-no-Kami|ja|豊受姫命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=draft}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami|ja|大己貴命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|ja|火産霊神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sarutahiko-no-Kami|ja|猿田彦命|12=draft}}
== History == A surviving '''munafuda''' (棟札, ridge plaque) records that the shrine underwent repairs in '''Keichō 16 (1611)''', indicating that it was founded even earlier.
A massive '''cryptomeria tree''', with a circumference of '''8 meters''', is located within the shrine precincts, demonstrating its long history.
According to the '''munafuda''', the current '''honden''' (main hall) was rebuilt in '''Bunka 4 (1801)''' by master carpenter '''Nakamura Denzaemon Zenju''' from '''Miyanokoshi-juku'''. It is made entirely of '''keyaki wood''' and features exquisite carvings of '''eagles, cranes, dragons, koi fish, immortals, and figures from Takasago'''.
In '''Meiji 5 (1872)''', the shrine was designated a '''village shrine''' (村社), and in '''Meiji 41 (1908)''', the nearby '''Sarutobi Shrine''' (founded in Keichō 16) was merged into it.
A further '''merger in Meiji 42 (1909)''' incorporated deities such as '''Izanagi, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime, Toyouke-hime, Susanoo, Ōkuninushi, Kagutsuchi, and Sarutahiko'''.
Before World War II, it was designated a '''shinsen-heihaku-ryō shrine''', meaning it received state-sponsored offerings during '''Kinensai''', '''Niinamesai''', and '''Reisai''' ceremonies.
Following the '''Shinto Directive''' of '''Shōwa 21 (1946)''', the shrine transitioned into a '''religious corporation'''.
A '''400-year-old cryptomeria tree''', which once stood '''53 meters tall''' and had a '''circumference of 3.8 meters''', was cut down in '''Shōwa 40 (1965)''' to expand '''Route 19'''.
The area was originally part of '''Yamaguchi Village, Kiso District, Nagano Prefecture''', but due to a '''cross-prefectural merger on February 13, 2005''', it is now part of '''Nakatsugawa City, Gifu Prefecture'''.
On '''June 3, 1993 (Heisei 5)''', the '''Yamaguchi Village Board of Education''' designated the '''honden''' as a '''tangible cultural property''', which is now registered under '''Nakatsugawa City'''.
== Festivals == The annual festival is held on '''July 26 and 27''', with the '''main festival (Honmatsuri) on July 27''' featuring the '''Hanamatsuri (Flower Horse Festival)'''.
From '''July 20''', two villages within the former '''Yamaguchi Village''' take turns crafting '''flower decorations''' for the horses.
Records indicate that in '''Kyōhō 2 (1717)''', the '''Hanamatsuri''' was already being held at '''Nangū Shrine in Tadai Village''', suggesting that the practice was introduced to the area at that time.
The '''flower decorations''' mimic '''chrysanthemums''' and '''susuki grass''', with each household receiving one for '''pest control in their fields'''.
During the two-day festival, '''4th and 5th grade girls''' perform a '''sacred dance''' that has been passed down since the '''Edo period'''.
Before '''World War II''', young men undergoing their '''military draft examination''' were responsible for '''organizing the festival'''.
On '''July 26''', the '''portable shrine (mikoshi)''' is paraded around the shrine precinct five times, and on '''July 27''', it is carried seven times.
== Insect Exorcism (Mushi Okuri) == Until '''Shōwa 32 (1957)''', an '''insect-exorcism ritual''' was performed on '''July 28''', the day after the festival.
Farmers would capture '''pests such as stink bugs, fleas, flies, and rice weevils''', wrap them in '''paper along with rice grains''', and tie them to '''bamboo branches''' adorned with '''colored paper strips'''.
The '''bamboo was then carried to Suwa Shrine''' for purification before being thrown into the '''Kiso River from Amagoi Bridge'''.
The practice was abandoned due to the '''construction of Yamaguchi Dam''', which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about '''pollution'''.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a '''kaguraden''', '''worship hall''', and '''Awa Shrine'''.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features '''warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki''', despite its '''inland, cold-climate location'''.
A '''rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)''' is also present.
In '''Taishō 14 (1925)''', a teacher from '''Matsumoto Women's Normal School''' declared it a '''one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection''', submitting a petition to the '''Ministry of Home Affairs'''.
Originally designated a '''Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture''', the shrine forest was '''transferred to Gifu Prefecture''' after the '''2005 merger''', and was officially designated as a '''Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006'''.
== References == * '''Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2''' (1995) * '''Shinano Temples & Shrines''' (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]

The shrine was founded as a branch shrine of '''Suwa Taisha''' in '''Shinano Province''' and enshrines a sacred mirror as its object of worship. The shrine crest is '''Kaji no Ha''' (梶の葉).

On '''June 3, 1993 (Heisei 5)''', the '''Yamaguchi Village Board of Education''' designated the '''honden''' as a '''tangible cultural property''', which is now registered under '''Nakatsugawa City'''.
== Festivals == The annual festival is held on '''July 26 and 27''', with the '''main festival (Honmatsuri) on July 27''' featuring the '''Hanamatsuri (Flower Horse Festival)'''.
From '''July 20''', two villages within the former '''Yamaguchi Village''' take turns crafting '''flower decorations''' for the horses.
Records indicate that in '''Kyōhō 2 (1717)''', the '''Hanamatsuri''' was already being held at '''Nangū Shrine in Tadai Village''', suggesting that the practice was introduced to the area at that time.
The '''flower decorations''' mimic '''chrysanthemums''' and '''susuki grass''', with each household receiving one for '''pest control in their fields'''.
During the two-day festival, '''4th and 5th grade girls''' perform a '''sacred dance''' that has been passed down since the '''Edo period'''.
Before '''World War II''', young men undergoing their '''military draft examination''' were responsible for '''organizing the festival'''.
On '''July 26''', the '''portable shrine (mikoshi)''' is paraded around the shrine precinct five times, and on '''July 27''', it is carried seven times.
== Insect Exorcism (Mushi Okuri) == Until '''Shōwa 32 (1957)''', an '''insect-exorcism ritual''' was performed on '''July 28''', the day after the festival.
Farmers would capture '''pests such as stink bugs, fleas, flies, and rice weevils''', wrap them in '''paper along with rice grains''', and tie them to '''bamboo branches''' adorned with '''colored paper strips'''.
The '''bamboo was then carried to Suwa Shrine''' for purification before being thrown into the '''Kiso River from Amagoi Bridge'''.
The practice was abandoned due to the '''construction of Yamaguchi Dam''', which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about '''pollution'''.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a '''kaguraden''', '''worship hall''', and '''Awa Shrine'''.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features '''warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki''', despite its '''inland, cold-climate location'''.
A '''rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)''' is also present.
In '''Taishō 14 (1925)''', a teacher from '''Matsumoto Women's Normal School''' declared it a '''one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection''', submitting a petition to the '''Ministry of Home Affairs'''.
Originally designated a '''Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture''', the shrine forest was '''transferred to Gifu Prefecture''' after the '''2005 merger''', and was officially designated as a '''Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006'''.
== References == * '''Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2''' (1995) * '''Shinano Temples & Shrines''' (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]

revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name= Suwa Shrine (Nakatsugawa, Yamaguchi) (諏訪神社) |image = {{image requested|cat=Nakatsugawa, Gifu}} |location= {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan |coordinates= {{Coord|35|33|47|N|137|32|24|E|region:JP-21_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |established= |main_hall= |festival= |shinto_rituals= }}
'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is a '''Shinto shrine''' located in {{ill|Nakatsugawa, Gifu|ja|中津川市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Gifu Prefecture, Japan.
The shrine was founded as a branch shrine of '''Suwa Taisha''' in '''Shinano Province''' and enshrines a sacred mirror as its object of worship. The shrine crest is '''Kaji no Ha''' (梶の葉).
== Enshrined Deities == === Main deity === * {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
=== Additional Deities === * {{ill|Izanagi-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉諾命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Izanami-no-Mikoto|ja|伊弉冉命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Toyouke-hime-no-Kami|ja|豊受姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素戔嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagutsuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sarutahiko-no-Kami|ja|猿田彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== History == A surviving '''munafuda''' (棟札, ridge plaque) records that the shrine underwent repairs in '''Keichō 16 (1611)''', indicating that it was founded even earlier.
A massive '''cryptomeria tree''', with a circumference of '''8 meters''', is located within the shrine precincts, demonstrating its long history.
According to the '''munafuda''', the current '''honden''' (main hall) was rebuilt in '''Bunka 4 (1801)''' by master carpenter '''Nakamura Denzaemon Zenju''' from '''Miyanokoshi-juku'''. It is made entirely of '''keyaki wood''' and features exquisite carvings of '''eagles, cranes, dragons, koi fish, immortals, and figures from Takasago'''.
In '''Meiji 5 (1872)''', the shrine was designated a '''village shrine''' (村社), and in '''Meiji 41 (1908)''', the nearby '''Sarutobi Shrine''' (founded in Keichō 16) was merged into it.
A further '''merger in Meiji 42 (1909)''' incorporated deities such as '''Izanagi, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime, Toyouke-hime, Susanoo, Ōkuninushi, Kagutsuchi, and Sarutahiko'''.
Before World War II, it was designated a '''shinsen-heihaku-ryō shrine''', meaning it received state-sponsored offerings during '''Kinensai''', '''Niinamesai''', and '''Reisai''' ceremonies.
Following the '''Shinto Directive''' of '''Shōwa 21 (1946)''', the shrine transitioned into a '''religious corporation'''.
A '''400-year-old cryptomeria tree''', which once stood '''53 meters tall''' and had a '''circumference of 3.8 meters''', was cut down in '''Shōwa 40 (1965)''' to expand '''Route 19'''.
The area was originally part of '''Yamaguchi Village, Kiso District, Nagano Prefecture''', but due to a '''cross-prefectural merger on February 13, 2005''', it is now part of '''Nakatsugawa City, Gifu Prefecture'''.
On '''June 3, 1993 (Heisei 5)''', the '''Yamaguchi Village Board of Education''' designated the '''honden''' as a '''tangible cultural property''', which is now registered under '''Nakatsugawa City'''.
== Festivals == The annual festival is held on '''July 26 and 27''', with the '''main festival (Honmatsuri) on July 27''' featuring the '''Hanamatsuri (Flower Horse Festival)'''.
From '''July 20''', two villages within the former '''Yamaguchi Village''' take turns crafting '''flower decorations''' for the horses.
Records indicate that in '''Kyōhō 2 (1717)''', the '''Hanamatsuri''' was already being held at '''Nangū Shrine in Tadai Village''', suggesting that the practice was introduced to the area at that time.
The '''flower decorations''' mimic '''chrysanthemums''' and '''susuki grass''', with each household receiving one for '''pest control in their fields'''.
During the two-day festival, '''4th and 5th grade girls''' perform a '''sacred dance''' that has been passed down since the '''Edo period'''.
Before '''World War II''', young men undergoing their '''military draft examination''' were responsible for '''organizing the festival'''.
On '''July 26''', the '''portable shrine (mikoshi)''' is paraded around the shrine precinct five times, and on '''July 27''', it is carried seven times.
== Insect Exorcism (Mushi Okuri) == Until '''Shōwa 32 (1957)''', an '''insect-exorcism ritual''' was performed on '''July 28''', the day after the festival.
Farmers would capture '''pests such as stink bugs, fleas, flies, and rice weevils''', wrap them in '''paper along with rice grains''', and tie them to '''bamboo branches''' adorned with '''colored paper strips'''.
The '''bamboo was then carried to Suwa Shrine''' for purification before being thrown into the '''Kiso River from Amagoi Bridge'''.
The practice was abandoned due to the '''construction of Yamaguchi Dam''', which altered the river's flow and caused concerns about '''pollution'''.
== Shrine Precincts == The shrine grounds include a '''kaguraden''', '''worship hall''', and '''Awa Shrine'''.
== Sacred Grove (Shasō) == The shrine’s forest features '''warm-temperate species like Aritaki and Sakaki''', despite its '''inland, cold-climate location'''.
A '''rare "Shidare-Enoki" (weeping hackberry tree)''' is also present.
In '''Taishō 14 (1925)''', a teacher from '''Matsumoto Women's Normal School''' declared it a '''one-of-a-kind specimen worthy of national protection''', submitting a petition to the '''Ministry of Home Affairs'''.
Originally designated a '''Natural Monument of Nagano Prefecture''', the shrine forest was '''transferred to Gifu Prefecture''' after the '''2005 merger''', and was officially designated as a '''Gifu Prefectural Natural Monument on September 5, 2006'''.
== References == * '''Yamaguchi Village History, Volume 2''' (1995) * '''Shinano Temples & Shrines''' (Shinano Mainichi Shimbun, 1981)
== External Links == * [https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html Gifu Prefecture Official Site] * [https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352 Gifu Shrine Directory]

revision.comment: 21 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:諏訪神社 (中津川市山口) up to rev 103078375
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 諏訪神社 (中津川市山口) |画像 = {{画像募集中|cat=中津川市}} |所在地= [[岐阜県]][[中津川市]][[山口村 (長野県)|山口]]731 |緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 33 | 緯度秒 = 47 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N |経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 32 | 経度秒 = 24 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神= [[建御名方神]]・[[伊弉諾命]]・[[伊弉冉命]]・[[応神天皇|誉田別命]]・[[木花咲耶姫]] |社格= |創建= |本殿= |例祭= |神事= }} '''諏訪神社'''（すわじんじゃ）は、[[岐阜県]][[中津川市]][[山口村 (長野県)|山口]]に鎮座する[[神社]]。
[[信濃国]]の[[諏訪大社]]から勧請して創建された神社で、御神体は鏡、社紋は[[梶の葉]]である。 == 祭神 == ;主神 *[[建御名方神]] ;配神 *[[伊弉諾命]] *[[伊弉冉命]] *[[木花咲耶姫]] *[[倉稲魂命]] *[[豊受姫命]] *[[大山祇命]] *[[素戔嗚尊]] *[[大己貴命]] *[[火産霊神]] *[[猿田彦命]]
== 歴史 == 現存する棟札に、[[慶長]]16年([[1611年]])に修復したことが記されているので、それ以前に創建されたことは明らかである。
社叢の目通しが周囲8mの老杉があり、その由緒の古さを物語っている。
棟札によると、現在の社殿は、[[文化 (元号)|文化]]4年([[1801年]])に再建されたもので、[[宮ノ越宿]]の[[宮大工]]の '''中村傳左衛門善重'''の作とある。
総[[ケヤキ|欅]]造の本殿は、流れ造りの二面二間で、鷲、鶴、龍、鯉、仙人、高砂の姥などの精巧華麗な彫刻が施されている。
[[明治]]5年([[1872年]])1月に旧[[村社]]となり、明治41年([[1908年]])7月には、旧村社の猿投神社<ref>旧山口村十一区鎮座、口碑に慶長十六勧請とある</ref>を合祀した。
明治42年([[1909年]])、神社統合によって、伊弉諾命・伊弉冉命・木花咲耶姫・豊受姫命・素戔嗚尊・大己貴命・火産霊神・猿田彦命などの神々が合祀された。
戦前までは、神饌幣帛料供進神社<ref>郷社、村社を対象に明治から終戦に至るまで勅令に基づき県令をもって県知事から、祈年祭、新嘗祭、例祭に神饌幣帛料を供進された神社。</ref>として祭祀を執行してきたが、
[[昭和]]21年([[1946年]])の[[神道指令]]により、[[宗教法人]]として運営されている。
かつて社地には、推定樹齢400年という杉の大木があった。その廻りは3.8m余、長さは53m余であったが、
昭和40年([[1965年]])9月、[[国道19号線]]の拡幅工事の際に切り倒された。
当地は元は、[[長野県]][[木曽郡]][[山口村 (長野県)|山口村]]であったが、[[平成]]17年([[2005年]])2月13日に岐阜県へ[[越県合併]]したため、現在は岐阜県中津川市山口となっている。
[[平成]]5年([[1993年]])6月3日、山口村教育委員会によって諏訪神社本殿が[[有形文化財]]に指定されたが、現在は岐阜県中津川市の有形文化財に指定されている。 == 祭礼 == 例祭は、7月26日と7月27日に固定されており、7月27日が本祭で花馬祭り(山車)が行われている。
7月20日になると山口村の中で当番が当たった二つの集落が[[花馬]]に付ける花作りを担当する。
[[享保]]2年([[1717年]])に[[田立村]]の南宮神社で花馬祭りを斎行した記録が有り、周辺地域の神社も相次いで花馬祭りを行うようになったことから、この頃から始められたと考えられる。
花は[[キク]]と[[ススキ]]を模り、一軒に一本づつ行き渡るように家の数だけ作る。花は祭りの後に家に持ち帰って畑の虫除けに立てた。花作り以外の集落は、神社周辺の掃除と馬場作りを行う。
祭の2日間は、小学校4～5年生の女子によって舞が奉納されるが、この舞作法は、[[馬籠宿|馬籠]]荒町の'''宮口茂穂'''神官から習い受けたものを毎年後輩に伝えて今日に至ったもので、舞の際に演奏される奏楽とともに継承されてきたものである。
[[太平洋戦争]]の終戦以前は、7月26日と7月27日の両日とも祭りの行事は、その年の[[徴兵検査]]にあたる若者によって執り行われていた。
御神体は、これらの若連が奏でる奏楽の中を、神官を従えて出御する。花馬の後には子供達が従った。
7月26日には五回、7月27日には七回、拝殿の周囲を回った。この後、花馬は神社前の国道19号線を約40m程、5回ないし7回走らせる放馬の神事が行われていたが、交通量が増えるに従って取りやめられた。
7月27日の本祭には[[獅子舞]]が出るが、乳児を獅子に咬んでもらうと健康に育つと言われている。
また、花馬から花を取って畑に挿せば、野菜に虫が付かないと言われており、祭りの参加者による花の奪い合いは壮観である。 ===虫送り=== 過去には、祭りが終わった翌日の7月28日に、[[虫送り]]が行われていた。
家や土蔵を払い清めた後に、[[シンクイムシ]]・[[ノミ]]・[[ハエ]]・[[コクゾウムシ]]などの虫を捕らえて米粒とともに紙に包み、捻って葉の付いた竹の枝に結び付けた。
竹には色短冊を付けて飾りつけ、これを諏訪神社へ持っていってお祓いを受けた後に、子供達が雨乞橋から[[木曽川]]へ流していたが、[[山口ダム (長野県)|山口ダム]]の出現によって、川の流れに変化が出て、竹の投棄は公害を生ずるおそれがあるということから、
昭和32年([[1957年]])を最後に、この行事は消滅した。 == 境内 == 神楽殿、拝殿、粟島神社がある。 === 社叢 === この社叢の植生は特徴的で、岐阜県と長野県境という内陸寒冷地に位置するにもかかわらず、[[アラカシ]]や[[サカキ]]等の暖帯性の樹木が生育している。
これは、この社叢が地下水の湧き出る場所にあるため、植物にとって大切な根の周りの温度が一定以下にならないためだと考えられている。
また、植生ばかりでなく珍木である「シダレエノキ」が存在することもこの社叢の価値を高めている。
[[大正]]14年（[[1925年]]）に、ここを訪れた[[長野師範学校#松本女子師範学校|松本女子師範学校]]の教諭が、この木を見て、「天下に類をみない珍種であり[[天然記念物]]に指定すべきである」と当時の[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]に申請したという逸話も残っている。
これらの理由によって社叢は、かつては長野県の天然記念物に指定されていたが、
当地は、[[平成]]17年([[2005年]])2月13日に岐阜県へ越県合併したため、平成18年([[2006年]])9月5日に岐阜県の天然記念物に指定された。 == 粟嶋神社 == ===祭神=== ;主神 *[[少彦名命]] ;配神 *[[天照皇太神]] *[[応神天皇|誉田別命]] *[[倉稲魂命]] *[[大山祗命]] ===歴史=== 明治42年([[1909年]])、社殿は、神社統合の際に、下山口にあった「猿投神社」を移転改築したものである。
この神社は相殿<ref>同じ社殿に2柱以上の神を合わせて祀ること。または、その社殿。</ref>として、天照皇太神・誉田別命・倉稲魂命・少彦名命・大山祗命を合祀している。
粟嶋信仰は、婦人病に効験ありとされており、特に安産の神、子宝の神として更には縁結びの神として崇められている。 ===祭礼=== 4月3日に行われており、安産につながることを意味して、底の無い竹柄杓が参拝者から奉納される。
また山口地区の伝統芸能保存会の子供たちによる奏楽・巫女の神楽が奉納されている。 == 関連リンク == *[https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html 上山口の諏訪神社社叢 岐阜県公式ホームページ] *[https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352&shrname=%E2%96%A0%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E2%96%A0 諏訪神社詳細 岐阜県神社庁] == 参考文献 == *『山口村誌 下巻(近・現代・民俗)』第六章 民俗 第六節 信仰 4 氏神 p750～p762 山口村誌編纂委員会 平成7年 *『山口村誌 下巻(近・現代・民俗)』第六章 民俗 第六節 信仰 5 粟島神社 p762 山口村誌編纂委員会 平成7年 *『山口村誌 下巻(近・現代・民俗)』第六章 民俗 第六節 信仰 6 神役 p763～p764 山口村誌編纂委員会 平成7年 *『寺と神社 (信州の文化シリーズ)』 諏訪神社 p252 信州郷土史研究会 [[信濃毎日新聞|信濃毎日新聞社]] 1981年
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
{{shinto-stub}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[諏訪神社 (中津川市山口)]] to [[Suwa Shrine (Nakatsugawa)]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 諏訪神社 (中津川市山口) |画像 = {{画像募集中|cat=中津川市}} |所在地= [[岐阜県]][[中津川市]][[山口村 (長野県)|山口]]731 |緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 33 | 緯度秒 = 47 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N |経度度 = 137 |経度分 = 32 | 経度秒 = 24 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E |祭神= [[建御名方神]]・[[伊弉諾命]]・[[伊弉冉命]]・[[応神天皇|誉田別命]]・[[木花咲耶姫]] |社格= |創建= |本殿= |例祭= |神事= }} '''諏訪神社'''（すわじんじゃ）は、[[岐阜県]][[中津川市]][[山口村 (長野県)|山口]]に鎮座する[[神社]]。
[[信濃国]]の[[諏訪大社]]から勧請して創建された神社で、御神体は鏡、社紋は[[梶の葉]]である。 == 祭神 == ;主神 *[[建御名方神]] ;配神 *[[伊弉諾命]] *[[伊弉冉命]] *[[木花咲耶姫]] *[[倉稲魂命]] *[[豊受姫命]] *[[大山祇命]] *[[素戔嗚尊]] *[[大己貴命]] *[[火産霊神]] *[[猿田彦命]]
== 歴史 == 現存する棟札に、[[慶長]]16年([[1611年]])に修復したことが記されているので、それ以前に創建されたことは明らかである。
社叢の目通しが周囲8mの老杉があり、その由緒の古さを物語っている。
棟札によると、現在の社殿は、[[文化 (元号)|文化]]4年([[1801年]])に再建されたもので、[[宮ノ越宿]]の[[宮大工]]の '''中村傳左衛門善重'''の作とある。
総[[ケヤキ|欅]]造の本殿は、流れ造りの二面二間で、鷲、鶴、龍、鯉、仙人、高砂の姥などの精巧華麗な彫刻が施されている。
[[明治]]5年([[1872年]])1月に旧[[村社]]となり、明治41年([[1908年]])7月には、旧村社の猿投神社<ref>旧山口村十一区鎮座、口碑に慶長十六勧請とある</ref>を合祀した。
明治42年([[1909年]])、神社統合によって、伊弉諾命・伊弉冉命・木花咲耶姫・豊受姫命・素戔嗚尊・大己貴命・火産霊神・猿田彦命などの神々が合祀された。
戦前までは、神饌幣帛料供進神社<ref>郷社、村社を対象に明治から終戦に至るまで勅令に基づき県令をもって県知事から、祈年祭、新嘗祭、例祭に神饌幣帛料を供進された神社。</ref>として祭祀を執行してきたが、
[[昭和]]21年([[1946年]])の[[神道指令]]により、[[宗教法人]]として運営されている。
かつて社地には、推定樹齢400年という杉の大木があった。その廻りは3.8m余、長さは53m余であったが、
昭和40年([[1965年]])9月、[[国道19号線]]の拡幅工事の際に切り倒された。
当地は元は、[[長野県]][[木曽郡]][[山口村 (長野県)|山口村]]であったが、[[平成]]17年([[2005年]])2月13日に岐阜県へ[[越県合併]]したため、現在は岐阜県中津川市山口となっている。
[[平成]]5年([[1993年]])6月3日、山口村教育委員会によって諏訪神社本殿が[[有形文化財]]に指定されたが、現在は岐阜県中津川市の有形文化財に指定されている。 == 祭礼 == 例祭は、7月26日と7月27日に固定されており、7月27日が本祭で花馬祭り(山車)が行われている。
7月20日になると山口村の中で当番が当たった二つの集落が[[花馬]]に付ける花作りを担当する。
[[享保]]2年([[1717年]])に[[田立村]]の南宮神社で花馬祭りを斎行した記録が有り、周辺地域の神社も相次いで花馬祭りを行うようになったことから、この頃から始められたと考えられる。
花は[[キク]]と[[ススキ]]を模り、一軒に一本づつ行き渡るように家の数だけ作る。花は祭りの後に家に持ち帰って畑の虫除けに立てた。花作り以外の集落は、神社周辺の掃除と馬場作りを行う。
祭の2日間は、小学校4～5年生の女子によって舞が奉納されるが、この舞作法は、[[馬籠宿|馬籠]]荒町の'''宮口茂穂'''神官から習い受けたものを毎年後輩に伝えて今日に至ったもので、舞の際に演奏される奏楽とともに継承されてきたものである。
[[太平洋戦争]]の終戦以前は、7月26日と7月27日の両日とも祭りの行事は、その年の[[徴兵検査]]にあたる若者によって執り行われていた。
御神体は、これらの若連が奏でる奏楽の中を、神官を従えて出御する。花馬の後には子供達が従った。
7月26日には五回、7月27日には七回、拝殿の周囲を回った。この後、花馬は神社前の国道19号線を約40m程、5回ないし7回走らせる放馬の神事が行われていたが、交通量が増えるに従って取りやめられた。
7月27日の本祭には[[獅子舞]]が出るが、乳児を獅子に咬んでもらうと健康に育つと言われている。
また、花馬から花を取って畑に挿せば、野菜に虫が付かないと言われており、祭りの参加者による花の奪い合いは壮観である。 ===虫送り=== 過去には、祭りが終わった翌日の7月28日に、[[虫送り]]が行われていた。
家や土蔵を払い清めた後に、[[シンクイムシ]]・[[ノミ]]・[[ハエ]]・[[コクゾウムシ]]などの虫を捕らえて米粒とともに紙に包み、捻って葉の付いた竹の枝に結び付けた。
竹には色短冊を付けて飾りつけ、これを諏訪神社へ持っていってお祓いを受けた後に、子供達が雨乞橋から[[木曽川]]へ流していたが、[[山口ダム (長野県)|山口ダム]]の出現によって、川の流れに変化が出て、竹の投棄は公害を生ずるおそれがあるということから、
昭和32年([[1957年]])を最後に、この行事は消滅した。 == 境内 == 神楽殿、拝殿、粟島神社がある。 === 社叢 === この社叢の植生は特徴的で、岐阜県と長野県境という内陸寒冷地に位置するにもかかわらず、[[アラカシ]]や[[サカキ]]等の暖帯性の樹木が生育している。
これは、この社叢が地下水の湧き出る場所にあるため、植物にとって大切な根の周りの温度が一定以下にならないためだと考えられている。
また、植生ばかりでなく珍木である「シダレエノキ」が存在することもこの社叢の価値を高めている。
[[大正]]14年（[[1925年]]）に、ここを訪れた[[長野師範学校#松本女子師範学校|松本女子師範学校]]の教諭が、この木を見て、「天下に類をみない珍種であり[[天然記念物]]に指定すべきである」と当時の[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]に申請したという逸話も残っている。
これらの理由によって社叢は、かつては長野県の天然記念物に指定されていたが、
当地は、[[平成]]17年([[2005年]])2月13日に岐阜県へ越県合併したため、平成18年([[2006年]])9月5日に岐阜県の天然記念物に指定された。 == 粟嶋神社 == ===祭神=== ;主神 *[[少彦名命]] ;配神 *[[天照皇太神]] *[[応神天皇|誉田別命]] *[[倉稲魂命]] *[[大山祗命]] ===歴史=== 明治42年([[1909年]])、社殿は、神社統合の際に、下山口にあった「猿投神社」を移転改築したものである。
この神社は相殿<ref>同じ社殿に2柱以上の神を合わせて祀ること。または、その社殿。</ref>として、天照皇太神・誉田別命・倉稲魂命・少彦名命・大山祗命を合祀している。
粟嶋信仰は、婦人病に効験ありとされており、特に安産の神、子宝の神として更には縁結びの神として崇められている。 ===祭礼=== 4月3日に行われており、安産につながることを意味して、底の無い竹柄杓が参拝者から奉納される。
また山口地区の伝統芸能保存会の子供たちによる奏楽・巫女の神楽が奉納されている。 == 関連リンク == *[https://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/page/7411.html 上山口の諏訪神社社叢 岐阜県公式ホームページ] *[https://www.gifu-jinjacho.jp/syosai.php?shrno=3352&shrname=%E2%96%A0%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E2%96%A0 諏訪神社詳細 岐阜県神社庁] == 参考文献 == *『山口村誌 下巻(近・現代・民俗)』第六章 民俗 第六節 信仰 4 氏神 p750～p762 山口村誌編纂委員会 平成7年 *『山口村誌 下巻(近・現代・民俗)』第六章 民俗 第六節 信仰 5 粟島神社 p762 山口村誌編纂委員会 平成7年 *『山口村誌 下巻(近・現代・民俗)』第六章 民俗 第六節 信仰 6 神役 p763～p764 山口村誌編纂委員会 平成7年 *『寺と神社 (信州の文化シリーズ)』 諏訪神社 p252 信州郷土史研究会 [[信濃毎日新聞|信濃毎日新聞社]] 1981年
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
{{shinto-stub}}
